projects.projectOverview: A custom MVC framework built solo in C# and .NET Core, featuring a Razor-like templating language with support for loops, conditionals, and dynamic HTML generation. The template parser was built using ANTLR4 to define and process the grammar.
projects.projectBackground: Built as a solo school project with the goal of understanding how frameworks like ASP.NET MVC actually work under the hood — routing, template rendering, dependency injection and all. Rather than just using a framework, the assignment was to build one.

projectFeatures.title: Custom Templating Language
projectFeatures.description: A Razor-like syntax supporting loops, conditionals, and dynamic expressions, parsed using an ANTLR4 grammar.

projectFeatures.title: MVC Architecture
projectFeatures.description: Full Model-View-Controller pattern with routing, controllers, and view rendering.

projectFeatures.title: Routing System
projectFeatures.description: RESTful routing that maps incoming URLs to the correct controller and action.

projectFeatures.title: Dependency Injection
projectFeatures.description: Basic DI container for managing service dependencies across the framework.

projectDevelopmentProcess: Designed the overall framework architecture before writing any code.
Defined a formal grammar for the templating language using ANTLR4, supporting loops, conditionals, and variable interpolation.
Built the parser and HTML renderer on top of that grammar.
Implemented routing, controllers, views, and a basic dependency injection system.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Creating a grammar flexible enough to support real control flow (loops and conditionals) while remaining parseable by ANTLR4.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Iteratively designed the ANTLR4 grammar, testing edge cases in the templating syntax and adjusting the grammar rules until the parser handled all expected inputs correctly.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Mapping incoming HTTP requests to the correct controller and action in a flexible, RESTful way.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Built a route parser and dispatcher that matched URL patterns to registered routes and invoked the appropriate controller methods.

projectOutcomes.title: Working Framework
projectOutcomes.description: A functional MVC framework capable of handling routing, rendering dynamic templates, and managing dependencies.

projectOutcomes.title: Deep Understanding
projectOutcomes.description: Building this from scratch gave a much clearer picture of how production frameworks solve these problems.

projectLessons: Designing a formal grammar is harder than it looks — small ambiguities in the grammar rules cause real parsing problems.
Building a framework from scratch is one of the best ways to understand why existing frameworks make the design choices they do.
Solo projects require more upfront architecture planning than team projects where problems can be caught earlier.

projects.projectOverview: A WebXR application for annotating LiDAR point clouds, built with TypeScript, React, and Three.js. Designed for a real client as part of a school project. Users annotate 3D street scan data in an immersive VR environment, with height-based color mapping, automatic bounding box generation, and virtual cameras aligned to the original capture rig.
projects.projectBackground: The client needed a faster and more accurate way to annotate LiDAR point cloud data captured from vehicles driving through streets — data used to train autonomous vehicle AI models. Traditional 2D annotation tools were slow and unintuitive for inherently 3D data. Our team of three built a VR environment where annotators could work directly in 3D space.

projectFeatures.title: Automatic Bounding Boxes
projectFeatures.description: Users brush over a group of points and the system automatically fits an oriented bounding box around them using 2D PCA to determine the optimal yaw alignment, rather than requiring manual box construction.

projectFeatures.title: Height-Based Color Mapping
projectFeatures.description: Points are colored by their height off the ground, making it easier to visually distinguish roads, vehicles, and other objects in the scan.

projectFeatures.title: Virtual Camera Alignment
projectFeatures.description: The vehicle's real cameras are reconstructed in the 3D scene using extrinsic calibration data (position and rotation) and intrinsic calibration data (focal length and principal point), with a custom projection matrix so each virtual camera's frustum matches the real lens exactly.

projectFeatures.title: Annotation Tools
projectFeatures.description: Tools for selecting, labeling, and categorizing points and objects within the VR environment.

projectFeatures.title: Data Export
projectFeatures.description: Annotated data can be exported in formats compatible with AI training pipelines.

projectDevelopmentProcess: Worked directly with the client to understand their annotation workflow and data format.
Loaded and rendered LiDAR point cloud data inside a Three.js WebXR environment.
Implemented height-based color mapping to make the 3D structure of the data easier to read.
Built the brush selection system and automatic oriented bounding box generation using 2D PCA.
Resolved a coordinate system mismatch between the LiDAR data format and WebXR's axis conventions.
Reconstructed the vehicle's camera rig in 3D using extrinsic and intrinsic calibration data, building a custom projection matrix per camera.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Users needed to be able to select a group of points by brushing over them and have the system draw a correctly fitted bounding box automatically. Point clusters representing vehicles or pedestrians are rarely axis-aligned, so a simple min/max box produced poor results.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Implemented an oriented bounding box algorithm using 2D PCA on the horizontal plane to determine the principal axis of the point distribution. The optimal yaw angle is extracted from the primary eigenvector, the points are rotated into that aligned space, a standard AABB is computed, and the result is rotated back to world space. Pitch and roll are intentionally left at zero since objects in vehicle-captured LiDAR sit flat on the ground.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: The LiDAR point cloud data uses a Z-up coordinate system (Z is vertical, Y is forward) while WebXR uses a Y-up system (Y is vertical, Z is backward). A naive axis swap produced a mirrored scene rather than a correct one.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Wrote a coordinate conversion layer that maps the backend's Z to WebXR's Y, and negates the backend's Y into WebXR's Z. The negation was the non-obvious part — without it the scene loaded but was mirrored along one axis. The same conversion applies to annotation boxes being saved back, with an inverse transform.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: The point cloud data was captured by cameras mounted on a vehicle, and the annotation tool needed to show those camera views in the correct positions in the 3D scene so annotators could cross-reference image data with point geometry.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Loaded each camera's extrinsic calibration (position and quaternion rotation) and applied the same coordinate conversion as the point cloud so cameras land in the correct world position. For the projection, built a custom Three.js projection matrix from the intrinsic calibration data (focal lengths and principal point), giving each virtual camera a frustum that matches the real physical lens exactly.

projectOutcomes.title: Delivered to Client
projectOutcomes.description: The application was delivered to the client as a working prototype for VR-based point cloud annotation.

projectOutcomes.title: Faster Annotation
projectOutcomes.description: The automatic bounding box system significantly reduced the manual effort required per annotation compared to traditional tools.

projectLessons: Coordinate system conventions differ between data formats and rendering environments — a naive axis swap isn't always enough, negation matters too.
Working with a real client changes the dynamic considerably; requirements are less defined up front and change as they see the product evolve.
Automatic geometry fitting (bounding boxes) is a more interesting problem than it first appears when the point clusters aren't axis-aligned.

projects.projectOverview: A fully functional online poker platform with a Java Spring Boot backend and React + Vite frontend. Supports real-time multiplayer gameplay via WebSockets, user accounts, configurable tables, and complex pot distribution logic including side pots.
projects.projectBackground: A school team project (team of 4-5) where I was primarily responsible for the backend. The goal was a working online poker platform with real-time gameplay, user accounts, and correct poker rules including the parts most implementations get wrong — side pots.

projectFeatures.title: Real-Time Gameplay via WebSockets
projectFeatures.description: All game state updates are pushed to connected clients in real time over WebSockets, keeping every player's view in sync.

projectFeatures.title: Side Pot Logic
projectFeatures.description: Handles the full complexity of poker pot distribution including side pots when players are all-in with different stack sizes.

projectFeatures.title: User Accounts & Profiles
projectFeatures.description: Players can create accounts, customize profiles, and manage their funds.

projectFeatures.title: Configurable Tables
projectFeatures.description: Table creation with configurable buy-ins and blind settings.

projectDevelopmentProcess: Designed the backend architecture in Java with Spring Boot and PostgreSQL for persistence.
Implemented real-time game state synchronization using SignalR.
Built the poker game logic including hand evaluation, betting rounds, and pot distribution.
Implemented side pot calculation for all-in scenarios.
Deployed on Google Cloud with media storage via buckets.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Correctly distributing pots when multiple players are all-in with different stack sizes requires tracking multiple separate pots and determining the correct winner for each — one of the more complex rules in poker.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Built a dedicated pot management system that tracks each side pot separately and evaluates the correct eligible winners per pot. The logic handles most cases correctly; a small number of edge cases in split pot scenarios were identified but not fully resolved within the project timeline — the fix was known but ran out of time to implement.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Keeping all connected players' game views consistent in real time without race conditions or state drift.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Used SignalR to push authoritative game state from the server to all clients after each action, keeping the server as the single source of truth.

projectOutcomes.title: Fully Playable
projectOutcomes.description: The game is playable end-to-end with real-time multiplayer, user accounts, and correct poker rules for the vast majority of scenarios.

projectOutcomes.title: Complex Backend Logic
projectOutcomes.description: The pot distribution system handles the real complexity of poker, not just the happy path.

projectLessons: SignalR makes real-time server-to-client communication straightforward, but keeping server state authoritative and preventing race conditions still requires careful design.
Poker pot distribution is deceptively complex — side pots alone require a solid understanding of the rules before writing any code.
Knowing exactly how to fix a bug but not having time to implement it is a common reality; documenting the known issue and solution is better than pretending it doesn't exist.

projects.projectOverview: A digital polling platform built with .NET 8 and Razor Pages, supporting dynamic poll flows with multiple question types, role-based access control, and media integration via Google Cloud Storage.
projects.projectBackground: A school project built for an educational client. I was primarily responsible for the backend. The system needed to replace traditional polling methods with something that could handle structured, branching question flows and different user roles with different levels of access.

projectFeatures.title: Dynamic Poll Flows
projectFeatures.description: Admins build polls with subjects, sub-subjects, and branching question flows rather than simple linear question lists.

projectFeatures.title: Role-Based Access Control
projectFeatures.description: Separate roles for admins, poll facilitators, and participants, each with distinct permissions implemented via Identity Server.

projectFeatures.title: Multiple Question Types
projectFeatures.description: Supports open-ended, yes/no, and multiple-choice questions within the same poll flow.

projectFeatures.title: Media Integration
projectFeatures.description: Questions and answers can include images and video stored in Google Cloud Storage buckets.

projectDevelopmentProcess: Designed the backend architecture and data model for representing flexible poll flows.
Implemented role-based access control using Identity Server.
Built the poll flow logic to support branching and multiple question types.
Integrated Google Cloud Storage for media uploads and retrieval.
Deployed on Google Cloud.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Representing poll flows that can branch, contain sub-subjects, and support multiple question types in a relational database without the schema becoming unmanageable.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Designed a modular data model that separates the flow structure from the question content, allowing different question types to be added without restructuring the core schema.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Allowing admins to attach images and video to questions while keeping the application itself stateless.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Stored all media in Google Cloud Storage buckets and referenced them by URL in the database, keeping the application layer clean.

projectOutcomes.title: Delivered to Client
projectOutcomes.description: The system was delivered to the educational client as a working platform.

projectOutcomes.title: Flexible Architecture
projectOutcomes.description: The data model supports a wide range of poll configurations without code changes.

projectLessons: Designing a flexible data model upfront saves a lot of pain later — rigid schemas are hard to change once data exists.
Role-based access control needs to be designed into the architecture from the start, not bolted on afterward.
Keeping media out of the application layer and in dedicated storage is the right call even for smaller projects.

projects.projectTitle: ASOT Mortar Module: Arma 3 AI Mortar Team
projects.projectOverview: An Arma 3 addon that implements realistic AI-controlled mortar team behavior, including shoot-and-scoot tactics, dynamic firing position evaluation, and simulated fire missions with ranging shots and accuracy distribution. Integrates with the ZEN mission editor framework via custom editor modules and dialogs.
projects.projectBackground: A personal project built out of a passion for Arma 3 and game AI. The goal was to create mortar teams that behave realistically — moving between positions, evaluating terrain, and executing fire missions with the same procedure real crews use — rather than the static, scripted behavior typical of mission editor artillery.

projectFeatures.title: Shoot-and-Scoot Behavior
projectFeatures.description: Mortar teams autonomously deploy, fire a mission, pack up, relocate to cover, and repeat — mimicking real indirect fire tactics.

projectFeatures.title: Multi-Criteria Position Evaluation
projectFeatures.description: Firing positions are evaluated using raycasting for line-of-sight clearance, slope analysis, proximity to roads and tree lines, and water checks.

projectFeatures.title: Realistic Fire Mission Simulation
projectFeatures.description: Each fire mission includes ranging shots with progressively tightening offsets before fire-for-effect, with accuracy modeled as a probability distribution (70% near-hit, 20% moderate error, 10% wide miss).

projectFeatures.title: ZEN Module Integration
projectFeatures.description: Mission makers place editor modules to define areas of operation and target zones. A custom spawn dialog lets them configure mortar compositions, behavior mode, and area denial settings.

projectDevelopmentProcess: Designed the overall behavior architecture: spawn pipeline, position evaluation, fire mission cycle, and loop control.
Built a multi-criteria position evaluator using raycasting to find defensible firing positions within a defined area.
Implemented the fire mission cycle with ranging shots, accuracy simulation, and target memory to skip re-ranging on repeat engagements.
Integrated with BIS_fnc_unpackStaticWeapon and BIS_fnc_packStaticWeapon for realistic mortar deployment animations.
Built ZEN editor modules with custom dialogs for mission maker configuration.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Finding positions that are tactically viable — flat enough to deploy on, with clear overhead and horizontal clearance, away from roads and dense terrain — required checking multiple criteria efficiently across a potentially large area.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Built a grid-based evaluator with early-exit logic. Each candidate position is tested against slope, water, road proximity, tree density, and multiple raycasts for line-of-sight clearance. Positions that pass all checks are cached on the module for the team to select from at runtime.

projectChallenges.challenge: Integration with BIS Static Weapon System
projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Arma 3's built-in static weapon pack/unpack system uses asynchronous event callbacks, making it difficult to sequence reliably with the rest of the behavior loop.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Registered event handlers on the StaticWeaponUnpacked and StaticWeaponPacked events and used a polling mechanism on a shared namespace variable to synchronize the behavior loop with the BIS function's completion.

projectOutcomes.title: Fully Functional AI Behavior
projectOutcomes.description: Mortar teams autonomously complete full fire mission cycles — including terrain evaluation, deployment, ranging, fire-for-effect, takedown, and relocation — without any scripted waypoints.

projectOutcomes.title: Mission Editor Ready
projectOutcomes.description: ZEN module integration makes the system accessible to mission makers without any scripting knowledge.

projectLessons: Asynchronous event-driven APIs in game engines require careful synchronization when you need sequential behavior — polling shared state is sometimes the pragmatic solution.
Simulating realistic tactics in game AI is as much a design problem as a coding problem — understanding how mortar teams actually operate was a prerequisite to modeling it.
Raycasting for spatial queries is powerful but expensive; early-exit conditions and result caching are essential to keep evaluation time acceptable.

images.alt: Mortar team deploying in the field

projects.projectOverview: This portfolio, built with Angular SSR and self-hosted on a repurposed desktop running Linux. Served via a Node.js container behind an Nginx reverse proxy with Let's Encrypt TLS. Deployed automatically via a GitHub Actions pipeline on every push to main.
projects.projectBackground: Rather than deploying to a managed platform, the goal was to own the full stack — from the hardware to the TLS certificate. The infrastructure setup was as much a learning exercise as the Angular application itself.

projectFeatures.title: Self-Hosted Infrastructure
projectFeatures.description: Runs on a repurposed desktop machine, with Nginx on the host proxying to a Dockerized Node.js container.

projectFeatures.title: Automated Deployment
projectFeatures.description: GitHub Actions pipeline builds and verifies the project, then SSHes into the server to pull, rebuild, and restart the container on every push to main.

projectFeatures.title: TLS via Let's Encrypt
projectFeatures.description: HTTPS handled by Certbot with automatic certificate renewal.

projectFeatures.title: Angular SSR
projectFeatures.description: Server-side rendering via Angular Universal, chosen as a learning exercise rather than a strict requirement for a portfolio site.

projectDevelopmentProcess: Set up Linux on a repurposed desktop and configured port forwarding and DNS for the domain sanderworks.be.
Built the Angular application with SSR.
Configured Docker Compose to run the Node.js SSR server, bound to localhost only.
Set up Nginx on the host to handle TLS termination and reverse proxy to the container.
Configured a GitHub Actions pipeline for automated build verification and deployment via SSH.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Getting a reliable automated deployment pipeline working against a self-hosted server rather than a managed platform.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Used GitHub Actions with the appleboy SSH action to connect to the server on a non-standard port, pull the latest code, rebuild, and restart Docker. Server credentials are stored as GitHub Actions secrets.

projectChallenges.challengeDescription: Configuring Angular Universal SSR to run correctly inside a Docker container behind a reverse proxy.
projectChallenges.solutionDescription: Ran the compiled server.mjs directly with Node.js inside the container, with Nginx proxying external traffic to the container's internal port.

projectOutcomes.title: Fully Operational
projectOutcomes.description: The site is live at sanderworks.be with automated deployments on every push.

projectOutcomes.title: Full Stack Ownership
projectOutcomes.description: Every layer from hardware to application is configured and maintained manually.

projectLessons: SSR adds real complexity to deployment — the server environment matters in ways it doesn't for a static build.
A CI/CD pipeline against self-hosted hardware has more moving parts than managed platforms, but understanding those parts is worth it.
Building and running your own infrastructure gives you a much better intuition for what managed platforms are actually doing for you.
